fuzzy_prd:
Our command-line tool accepts two kinds of repeatable string options that users type at the prompt: HTTP header definitions and URL route mappings. Right now parsing is scattered and inconsistent. We need a small option-parsing library to clean this up. For headers: the user writes something like 'Header-Name: value', and we need that decoded into a map from name to value — the value is everything after the first colon, whitespace trimmed, and names should be handled case-insensitively so duplicates are collapsed. For route mappings: the user writes 'source=destination', and we split on the first unescaped equals sign — if someone needs a literal equals sign in the source or destination path they can escape it with a backslash. The library should accept a list of raw strings and produce a structured map, outputting an entry count followed by one line per entry in input order. Malformed mappings — missing separator, empty source, empty destination, or more than one unescaped separator — should fail with a descriptive error message that tells users exactly what the expected format is. Follow the same error-type convention used in the validation layer for other options in this project.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in on route mappings: when one of those is invalid, it throws an ArgumentException. The output is: 'error=ArgumentException\n' followed by 'message=The format of the key-value pair is invalid. It must contain exactly one \'=\' separator. Make sure non-separator \'=\' characters are escaped (\'\\=\').\n'.

Also, the validity rule here should be read pretty literally: a mapping is valid when, after trimming, it contains exactly one unescaped '=' with non-empty text on both sides. So we should reject the empty string, whitespace-only, no separator, empty left or right side, or more than one unescaped separator. And just to make the escaping behavior explicit, a '\=' sequence in the input is treated as a literal equals sign, not a separator. After splitting on the single unescaped '=', each escaped '\=' on both sides is converted back to a plain '=' in the result.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature:
IReadOnlyDictionary<string,string> ParseHeaders(IEnumerable<string> headers)
IReadOnlyDictionary<string,string> ParseRouteMap(IEnumerable<string> mappings)

trigger_keywords: header name case sensitivity in output
header name casing in the result map
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Header names are treated case-insensitively for deduplication purposes, but the original casing of each name is preserved in the output. The output uses the exact casing from the input string.
First line: 'count=N\n'. Then one line per entry: 'key => value\n'. Entries appear in the same order as the input strings.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same error-type convention used in the validation layer for other options
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The .NET ArgumentException class, which is the standard validation exception used throughout the dotnet-serve option-parsing layer. The error type name 'ArgumentException' appears literally in the error output line.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Refer to the src/ directory for the HeaderParser and RouteMapParser implementations. The rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ files show the exact expected output format for each scenario.

feature1_parse_headers.json.description: Parse a list of raw HTTP header definition lines into a header-name to header-value map. Each line uses a colon to separate the name from the value; the value is taken from everything after the first colon, with surrounding whitespace trimmed. Header names are matched case-insensitively. The output reports the number of resulting entries followed by one 'name => value' line per entry in input order.

feature2_1_route_map_valid.json.description: Parse a list of raw route-mapping strings into a source-pattern to destination-prefix map. Each string contains exactly one unescaped '=' separator; the part before it is the source pattern and the part after is the destination prefix. A literal equals sign inside either side is escaped by preceding it with a backslash, and each escaped equals is unescaped to a plain equals in the result. The output reports the number of resulting entries followed by one 'source => destination' line per entry in input order.

feature2_2_route_map_invalid.json.description: Reject malformed route-mapping strings. Before parsing, each string is trimmed of surrounding whitespace. A string is invalid when, after trimming, it does not contain exactly one unescaped '=' separator with a non-empty source on the left and a non-empty destination on the right: this covers strings with no separator, an empty side, only whitespace, or more than one unescaped separator. Parsing an invalid mapping aborts with an error; the output reports the error type name and its message.

cases.expected_output:
error=ArgumentException
message=The format of the key-value pair is invalid. It must contain exactly one '=' separator. Make sure non-separator '=' characters are escaped ('\=').
